core_takeaway: No first-position advantage is visible. Agent_1 secured Quill because it was uncontested, but the first formal proposal did not beat the last: Agent_1's proposal gave Agent_3 nothing and drew a rejection, while Agent_3's final proposal preserved all three top-value bundles and was accepted unanimously. The first and last positions both ended at maximum utility.
why_or_counterexample: The main counterexample is direct: Proposal #1 by Agent_1 received only 2 acceptances and 1 rejection, while Proposal #3 by Agent_3 received 3 acceptances and 0 rejections. Agent_1 did get Quill, but Agent_3 did not lose to the earlier position; the last proposal became the consensus because it gave Agent_3 Stone+Pencil while keeping Agent_1 and Agent_2 at full value.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by demanding Quill, then Agent_2 and Agent_3 quickly mapped out a non-conflicting split: Quill to Agent_1, Apple+Jewel to Agent_2, and Stone+Pencil to Agent_3. Publicly, the agents converged on that exact three-way allocation before formal proposals.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All three private-thinking entries recognized the same efficient split. Agent_1 wanted Quill, Agent_2 wanted Apple+Jewel, and Agent_3 wanted Stone+Pencil; Agent_3's private thinking also treated loss of Stone+Pencil as unacceptable because no equivalent swap existed.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Agent_1 and Agent_2 submitted identical proposals that protected Agent_1 and Agent_2 but left Agent_3 empty. Agent_3 submitted the previously discussed balanced split, which ultimately matched the final allocation.
phase_notes.private_voting: Agent_1 and Agent_2 accepted every proposal that preserved their own maximum payoff, but Agent_3 rejected Proposals #1 and #2 because they removed Stone+Pencil. Proposal #3 was the only one accepted by all three agents.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection section is present in the transcript, and the metadata reports zero reflection entries.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's opening focus on Quill helped lock in its sole valuable item, especially because the other agents placed no value on Quill. This supports a narrow form of first-position benefit, but only for securing a non-conflicting item rather than outperforming the last position.

The first formal proposal protected Agent_1 but excluded Agent_3 from all valued items. It failed to reach unanimity, while the last formal proposal was accepted by everyone, so proposal timing did not translate into first-position dominance.

mechanisms.theme: Last agent had a decisive veto
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3's Stone+Pencil bundle was worth its full utility, so any proposal leaving Agent_3 empty was unacceptable. This made the last position robust: Agent_3 could reject earlier allocations and support the proposal that delivered its top bundle.

evidence.quote: I highly value Pencil (64) and Stone (36), totaling 100.
leaves me without any items I value

mechanisms.explanation: The public and private record shows a clear Pareto split available from the beginning. The final result followed that efficient allocation rather than giving an extra edge to whoever spoke or proposed first.

evidence.quote: This is exactly the package Agent_2 described
aligns with each agent's top-valued items.
